did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with the EfficientNetB0 architecture, to classify melanoma skin lesions from dermoscopy images. The system also employed Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for image enhancement and was deployed on a Streamlit web app with Jetson Orin Nano for inference.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a web-based melanoma classification system using EfficientNetB0 and Streamlit. It describes a specific methodology (data collection, CNN training, CLAHE enhancement), reports new empirical results (accuracy up to 100% on a public dataset), and includes a workflow and experimental results section, distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: Web-App based Melanoma Skin Cancer Classification through Streamlit and Deep Learning
journal: 2024 IEEE 13th Global Conference on Consumer Electronics (GCCE)
brief_summary: This study developed a web-based application for classifying melanoma skin lesions using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with the EfficientNetB0 architecture. The system utilized Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for image enhancement and was deployed on a Streamlit platform with Jetson Orin Nano for edge inference. The model was trained and tested on a public dataset containing 1,000 samples each of normal and melanoma skin images. Evaluation results demonstrated that the EfficientNetB0 model achieved an accuracy of up to 100%, along with perfect precision, recall, and F1 scores. The research concludes that the proposed system effectively classifies skin lesions and performs well on edge devices.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 1,000 samples of melanoma images. The study focuses on classifying melanoma skin cancer.
The second class in the binary classification task, serving as the negative class for melanoma. The dataset contained 1,000 samples of normal skin images.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'normal skin' and 'melanoma' classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification of skin lesions' and 'classification of melanoma skin'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (normal vs. melanoma) using a CNN (EfficientNetB0) on a dataset with two classes (1,000 samples each). The reported metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, with results reaching up to 100% accuracy for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: USK Normal Skin Dataset and ISIC 2020 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A combined public dataset used for the study. It consists of normal skin images sourced from the USK Normal Skin Dataset (https://ademift.github.io/USK-NORMAL-SKIN-DATASET/) and melanoma images sourced from the ISIC 2020 Challenge dataset (https://challenge-isic-archive.com/data/2020).

use.training: Used to train the EfficientNetB0 CNN model.
use.testing: Used to test the system's classification accuracy and evaluate performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score.

EfficientNetB0 (with CLAHE).notes: CLAHE enhancement led to higher accuracy in every architecture compared to original images.

USK Normal Skin Dataset and ISIC 2020 Da.notes: The paper reports that the EfficientNetB0 model achieved an accuracy of up to 100%, along with perfect precision, recall, and F1 scores. Table 1 shows sample classification accuracies ranging from 99.18% to 100% for normal and melanoma classes. The conclusion states the application achieved an accuracy of up to 100%.

training_details.preprocessing: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for image enhancement

preprocessing_techniques: Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for image enhancement
conclusions: The study successfully developed a web-based application for classifying skin lesions (normal vs. melanoma) using the EfficientNetB0 Convolutional Neural Network architecture, deployed via Streamlit on a Jetson Orin Nano edge device.

The EfficientNetB0 model achieved an accuracy of up to 100%, along with perfect precision, recall, and F1 scores, when tested on a public dataset comprising 1,000 normal skin images and 1,000 melanoma images.
The application demonstrated high classification accuracy on individual sample sets, with accuracy scores ranging from 99.18% to 100% for both normal and melanoma classes.

The use of Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) for image enhancement resulted in higher accuracy across all tested architectures, indicating that CLAHE is directly proportional to classification accuracy.
The proposed system is capable of running effectively on edge devices (Jetson Orin Nano), suggesting its potential for practical deployment and future upgrades.
important_remarks: The reported accuracy of up to 100% (perfect precision, recall, and F1 scores) is highly suspicious and likely indicates data leakage, overfitting, or a trivially easy dataset split, as such perfect performance is rarely achievable in real-world melanoma classification tasks.

The dataset is artificially constructed by combining 1,000 normal skin images from the USK Normal Skin Dataset with 1,000 melanoma images from the ISIC 2020 dataset, rather than using a balanced, naturally occurring clinical dataset, which introduces significant population and dataset bias.
The study lacks external validation on independent, unseen clinical datasets, limiting the generalizability and clinical relevance of the findings.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the robustness of the results.
The paper does not report key training details such as the optimizer, loss function, learning rate, or number of epochs, making reproducibility difficult.

The evaluation appears to be based on a small number of sample sets (e.g., Table 1 shows results for only three sets of images) rather than a comprehensive test on the entire held-out test set, which may inflate performance metrics.

The study is a conference paper presented at a consumer electronics conference (GCCE), which may have less rigorous peer review standards compared to specialized medical imaging or dermatology journals.
